narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10, which is right around the typical level for students in our dataset. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 57.2%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision was driven primarily by a few key factors in your academic record that stood out compared to most applicants. Your LSAT score of 25 was the single biggest influence pushing the prediction toward failure, as it's notably lower than the average of about 37 seen in students who typically pass. Similarly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of around 5.5—significantly contributed to concerns about bar exam readiness, ranking as the next most important factor. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest counterbalancing effect, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other feature, while your plan to attend full-time also helped slightly, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Your Year 1 decile of 3 and middle family income bracket had minimal impact overall. Compared to typical students, your profile is strong in GPA and enrollment status but lags in LSAT and later-year performance, which tipped the scales for the model. While this is disappointing, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive academic paths, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, excelling in advanced coursework to improve your decile rankings, and gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and potential. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these areas—keep pushing forward, and we're rooting for you.
